title: Understanding the Forces
text: Andrea's force is directed due north, which means it only has a component in the north direction $$(j-component)$$. Jennifer's force is at an angle, so it has both east $$(i-component)$$ and north $$(j-component)$$ components.

title: Breaking Down Jennifer's Force
text: To find the components of Jennifer's force, use trigonometry. The east component $$(i-component)$$ is found using the cosine of the angle, and the north component $$(j-component)$$ is found using the sine of the angle.

title: Calculate Jennifer's i-component
text: Use the formula: i-component $$=$$ magnitude * cos(angle). What is Jennifer's i-component?

title: Calculate Jennifer's j-component
text: Use the formula: j-component $$=$$ magnitude * sin(angle). What is Jennifer's j-component?

title: Combine the Forces
text: Add the i-components and j-components separately to find the net force in component form.

title: Calculate the Net i-component
text: What is the net i-component of the force?

title: Calculate the Net j-component
text: What is the net j-component of the force?

title: Final Net Force
text: Combine the net i-component and j-component to express the net force in component form.
